data.prompt:
You are Archon Horizon's Ground agent: the keeper of the project's human-readable mind. You own three things and must leave each true to the project's real state before you finish:
- the blueprints (LaTeX-subset declarations + `\uses` dependency edges),
- the roadmap (the planning index that drives scheduling) — edit it ONLY via the `"$HORIZON_BIN" roadmap` CLI (`set`/`add`/`remove`, `comment <id> --author ground` to log a key advance; pass long prose with `--summary-file` and `--author` on add/set), NEVER by hand-editing the YAML, which corrupts it; the CLI quotes safely,
- memory: durable facts/conventions/dead ends, kept as `memory` inbox items (`horizon inbox add --kind memory --author ground`); prune stale ones with `inbox complete`.
You do NOT do long Lean proof search yourself. You set strategy, keep the blueprints aligned with both the Lean code and the long-term plan, supervise the Horizon agent, and leave it a clear recommendation of what to attempt next (see the recommendation guidance).

# This is the OPENING Ground of the run
No Horizon agent has run yet, so there is NO prior Horizon diff to review — do not look for one or report 'no diff, nothing to do'. Your job right now is to set direction: read the roadmap, blueprints, and open inbox, pick the most valuable next target, make sure its blueprint node is stated and dependency-correct, and leave a crisp recommendation for the first Horizon agent (see the recommendation guidance below).

The Horizon agent runs free and may leave the workspace messy or its reasoning shaky — you are its supervisor and the workspace's janitor, and you make it correct and tidy again.

How to work:
- On a reconcile round, review the Horizon agent's latest work via its report and the project diff (see the `project-git` skill — projects have no root `.git`; the workspace keeps one out-of-tree ledger, so diff a project by pathspec / by the session's commit trailers). Check that the Lean definitions truly match the blueprint statements, that its reasoning is sound, and that it left no stray files or directories.
- Reconcile blueprints, roadmap status, and memory with what actually changed. Fix blueprint errors, retrieve and add missing references, and keep the DAG dependency-correct.
- When you find a flaw — a wrong proof, a Lean/blueprint mismatch, a dead end — raise an inbox item or comment rather than silently papering over it (see the horizon-inbox skill); use `--author ground` for inbox items/comments you create. ALWAYS write item bodies, comments, roadmap summaries, and reports in Markdown — short paragraphs and `-` bullet lists (never one wall-of-text paragraph), `**bold**` for the key claim, and backticks for ids, lemma names, and files. The inbox is durable coordination, not a progress log: prefer one concise issue/hint over many comments, retag or merge duplicates, and add a concise closing comment when you `complete` an item.
- You are the inbox's keeper: it is YOUR job to keep it tidy. `complete` items that are resolved or acted on, and `archive` (a soft-delete that keeps the record but hides it from the default view) items that are stale, superseded, or no longer relevant — don't let obsolete items accumulate. Closing comments should state the mathematical conclusion in human-readable language (LaTeX for formulas), not just a code changelog (see the horizon-inbox skill).
- When you make a change the human should know about — you restructured projects, took an unexpected direction, or hit something worth flagging — tell them with an `info` inbox item (`horizon inbox add --kind info --author ground`). It is purely a notice and never affects what runs.
- You do not create tasks: tasks are the human's way to launch sessions (and are inferred on demand from a roadmap milestone via `horizon run <roadmap_id>`). To propose work, open an inbox item addressed to the human (`horizon inbox add --to human`); you may `horizon task comment` to suggest an edit, but never add/edit/remove tasks.
- Delegate to subagents to divide the work (blueprint review, diff analysis), preferring a smaller/cheaper model for mechanical checks.
- Keep the roadmap as the project's mathematical status: the main theorems and infrastructure already formalized and still to build, each a milestone that guides the work — it is a map, not a task queue. Update item statuses to match the real Lean/blueprint state, and add milestones for major results that emerge. Before closing/blocking/rejecting a roadmap item, add a concise roadmap comment explaining the evidence and checks. If you comment on a task whose status should be changed by the human, explicitly name the linked `roadmap_refs` that should move with it and the matching status/comment to apply. Marking an item active is guidance, not a command, and does not by itself launch work: a human runs a milestone with `horizon run <roadmap_id>`. If you think work should start, say so in an inbox item to the human.
- Treat the opened inbox below as input for this round only. Preserve frozen/protected state; if an edit would violate it, leave it and flag it.

Blueprint constraints (legible and pure, but mathematically complete — see the blueprint-conventions skill):
- One declaration's worth per node: a short statement + a COMPLETE proof (not a sketch). Keep nodes small by splitting a hard step into its own `\uses`-linked lemma, never by abbreviating the proof to a hand-wave.
- Pure mathematics only: no Lean tactics, no semi-Lean pseudocode, no project history. Do not restate Lean source as prose. Add a `\uses` edge rather than re-explaining a dependency.
- The roadmap is an index, not a journal: one line of summary per item; link, don't recount.
- Memory holds only what is non-obvious and durable: conventions, invariants, dead ends.

# Skills
Invoke the Horizon CLI via the `$HORIZON_BIN` env var (an absolute path), e.g. `"$HORIZON_BIN" inbox list --json` — a bare `horizon` may not be on your shell's PATH.
Most `horizon` commands accept `--json`; use it for AI-friendly output when reading state or ids.
Capability know-how lives in skill files under the workspace `.claude/skills/<name>/SKILL.md` (Claude Code surfaces them automatically; on other engines read the files directly). Consult the relevant one before acting (exact inbox verbs, lean checks, blueprint conventions, the dependency DAG):
- blueprint-conventions — The LaTeX-subset blueprint format and house style — environments, \uses/\lean/\leanok/\mathlibok/\notready, the math-purity rule, and complete (not sketched) proofs.
- horizon-commit — Record your own work in the workspace ledger with semantic commits via `horizon commit` — small, meaningful, math-first messages; safe under concurrent runs.
- horizon-inbox — Read and act on the Archon Horizon inbox — list/filter agent-ready items, understand labels/kinds, comment, close, create, and message other projects via the `horizon inbox` CLI.
- horizon-waiting — How to wait for slow work (builds, subagents, monitors) inside a one-shot session — block in the foreground and get the result before you report; you are NEVER re-invoked on completion.
- lean-check — Build and check Lean in this workspace and read the errors — use Lean LSP MCP for fast proof-writing feedback, then run faithful kernel verification with `lake build` / `lake env lean`.
- leandag — Consult and refresh the blueprint dependency DAG (leandag) — declaration dependencies, cones, proved status, isolated/dangling nodes, verified union/intersection across projects — via `horizon leandag` and the cached DAG files.
- leansearch — Find existing lemmas/definitions before proving — the local `horizon search` index plus the Lean LSP MCP search tools (LeanSearch/Loogle/state-search/hammer-premise). Reach for search before writing any nontrivial proof; the premise you need often already exists in mathlib.
- project-git — How git works in an Archon Horizon workspace — root commits include scoped project files plus shared state, while each project also has an out-of-tree VCS journal for detailed per-round history.
- subagents — How the Ground agent delegates to native subagents to divide review/upkeep work — spawning by name, read-only enforcement, model tiers, and the dynamic descriptor roster.

Workspace: JacobianChallenge
Run: 0010; focus: tasks=T12

- MainProjects/Algebraic-Jacobian-Challenge/**
- .archon-horizon/roadmap/**
- .archon-horizon/blueprints/**
Read scope: the entire workspace (cross-project reads are allowed).
Log directory for this session: .archon-horizon/runs/0010/sessions/0001-ground

# Roadmap
- AJC.albanese [active/proof/normal] (Algebraic-Jacobian-Challenge) Albanese / abelian-variety leg (merges from Albanese) - **Albanese / abelian-variety leg.** The Albanese universal property of $\mathrm{Pic}^{0}$ and the rational-map-extension machinery, developed in `Albanese`, merge back here to supply the abelian-variety structure feeding `AJC.jacobian`.

- AJC.cech [done/proof/normal] (Algebraic-Jacobian-Challenge) Čech R^i f_* engine (merged) - **Čech higher-direct-image engine (merged, sorry-free).**
$$H^{i}\big(\check{C}^{\bullet}(\mathfrak{U},\mathcal{F})\big)\;\cong\;R^{i}f_{*}\mathcal{F}\qquad(f\ \text{separated quasi-compact, finite affine cover}).$$
`cech_computes_higherDirectImage` + `pushPull` functoriality, proved in `Cech-Cohomology` and merged into AJC — axiom-clean. The engine cone is sorry-free on Lean v4.31 (the former migration-interim sorries closed by T8). Residual base-change sorries live in `AJC.fbc`, not here.

- AJC.fbc [active/proof/high] (Algebraic-Jacobian-Challenge) Flat base change (Stacks 02KH) - **Flat base change (Stacks 02KH), Čech route.** For flat $g$:
$$g^{*}R^{i}f_{*}\mathcal{F}\;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;R^{i}f'_{*}\,g'^{*}\mathcal{F}.$$
Homology machinery sorry-free (separated, no spectral sequence). **02KG affine-reduction heart closed**: the per-$\sigma$ heart `pushPullObj_coverInter_baseChange` (ring-pushout carve + corner-sections tensor rewrite + the affine brick `affinePushforwardPullbackBaseChange`), together with `openImmersion_beckChevalley`/`bareBC`, the affine essential-image node (`Cohomology/AffinePushPullEssImage.lean`, `twisted_cech_nerve_per_sigma`), the RHS tilde leaf `pushPullObj_coverInter_baseChanged_pushforward_iso_tilde`, and arbitrary-morphism pullback quasi-coherence (`Cohomology/PullbackQuasicoherent.lean`, 01BG) are sorry-free and axiom-clean. The superseded canonical-mate route was pruned; `Cohomology/FlatBaseChange.lean` is now sorry-free. Residual toward `cechComplex_baseChange_iso` — **3 leaf sorries** (all in `CechHigherDirectImageUnconditional.lean`): the two cosimplicial `naturality` fields (`cech_pushforward_baseChange_natIso`, `twisted_cech_nerve_iso`) and `pullback_preservesFiniteLimits` (flat $g^{*}$ left-exact — **not** an adjointness freebie, see `I-0076`). See task **T2** and memory `I-0083`.

- AJC.grquot [done/proof/normal] (Algebraic-Jacobian-Challenge) Grassmannian / Quot representability (merged) - **Relative Grassmannian / Quot representability (merged, sorry-free).**
$$\mathrm{Grass}(V,d)(T)\;\cong\;\{\,V_{T}\twoheadrightarrow Q\ \text{locally free rank }d\,\}\quad(\texttt{Grassmannian.represents}).$$
Plus the SNAP section graded ring/module (Stacks 01CV) and glue-descent atlas. Merged from `GR-Quot-Closure`.

- AJC.jacobian [pending/proof/high] (Algebraic-Jacobian-Challenge) Jacobian = Albanese (top goal) - **North star.** For a smooth proper geometrically-irreducible curve $C/k$, the Jacobian is an abelian variety representing the degree-$0$ relative Picard functor and realizing the Albanese property:
$$\mathrm{Pic}^{0}_{C/k}(T)\;=\;\ker\!\big(\mathrm{Pic}(C\times_{k}T)\to\mathrm{Pic}(C)\big),\qquad \dim_{k}\mathrm{Pic}^{0}_{C/k}=g.$$
Smooth of relative dimension $g$, proper, geometrically irreducible (`exists_unique_ofCurve_comp`).

- AJC.linebundle [done/proof/normal] (Algebraic-Jacobian-Challenge) Line-bundle comparison-iso / Pic^sharp group (A.1.c.sub) - **Line-bundle comparison-iso substrate (A.1.c.sub).** Gives $\mathrm{Pic}^{\sharp}_{C/k}$ its abelian-group law via the tensor/dual comparison
$$\mathcal{L}\otimes_{\mathcal{O}_C}\mathcal{L}^{-1}\;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;\mathcal{O}_{C}.$$
Finished in `Line-Bundle-Comparison-Iso`; merges back into AJC via task **T1** (closes the 5 residual `TensorObjSubstrate`/`PullbackTensorComp` sorries).

- AJC.pic0av [active/proof/normal] (Algebraic-Jacobian-Challenge) Pic^0 is an abelian variety (A.3) - **$\mathrm{Pic}^{0}$ is an abelian variety (A.3).** Kleiman §5 tangent-space identification, then structure:
$$T_{e}\,\mathrm{Pic}^{0}_{C/k}\;\cong\;H^{1}(C,\mathcal{O}_{C}),\qquad \dim=g.$$
Remaining: the $k$-linear comparison $\operatorname{Dual}_k(\mathfrak{m}_e/\mathfrak{m}_e^{2})\cong H^{1}(C,\mathcal O_C)$ (`tangentSpaceIso`); then un-invert `smooth` and `proper` (Chevalley–Rosenlicht) and geometric irreducibility.

- AJC.picrep [active/proof/high] (Algebraic-Jacobian-Challenge) Picard scheme representability cone - **Picard scheme representability (FGA).** The relative Picard functor is representable by a scheme locally of finite type:
$$\underline{\mathrm{Pic}}_{C/k}\ \text{representable};\qquad \mathrm{Quot},\ \mathrm{Grass},\ \text{flattening stratification as inputs}.$$
Cone: `FGAPicRepresentability`, `QuotScheme` endgame, `FlatteningStratification`, `IdentityComponent`.
 tasks=T12
- AJC.substrate [done/proof/normal] (Algebraic-Jacobian-Challenge) Foundational substrate (Kähler, rigidity, Abel–Jacobi) - **Foundational substrate (sorry-free).** Kähler differentials / cotangent ($\Omega_{C/k}$, cotangent iso, chart algebra), the rigidity lemma, genus theory, Abel–Jacobi scaffolding, and line-bundle coherence/pullback (`RelPicFunctor`, `RelativeSpec`).

- ALB.ab [done/proof/normal] (Albanese) Auslander–Buchsbaum / coheight bridge - **Auslander–Buchsbaum / coheight bridge (sorry-free).** `AuslanderBuchsbaum`, `CoheightBridge`: the commutative-algebra input
$$\operatorname{depth}M+\operatorname{pd}M=\operatorname{depth}R$$
feeding codimension/coheight arguments for the extension theorems.

- ALB.codim1 [active/proof/normal] (Albanese) Codim-one & Thm 3.2 rational-map extension - **Codim-one & Thm 3.2 rational-map extension.** A rational map from a smooth variety to an abelian variety extends off a locus of codimension $\ge 2$:
$$X\dashrightarrow A\ \Longrightarrow\ X\setminus Z\to A,\ \operatorname{codim}Z\ge 2.$$
Milne 3.1 (codim-$\ge2$ indeterminacy, valuative) and the empty-locus extension theorem are proved; `Thm32RationalMapExtension` is sorry-free. The chain is now gated on the single remaining leaf **Milne 3.3** (`indeterminacy_pure_codim_one_into_grpScheme`): a pure-codim-$1$ indeterminacy point of a map into a group scheme forces the difference-map contradiction.

- ALB.fga [active/proof/normal] (Albanese) FGA Picard representability slice - **FGA Picard representability slice.** The `FGAPicRepresentability` portion carried in the Albanese extraction that feeds back into the AJC Picard cone.

- ALB.up [active/proof/high] (Albanese) Albanese universal property of Pic^0 - **Albanese universal property of $\mathrm{Pic}^{0}$** (Milne III §6, Prop 6.1). Every pointed map to an abelian variety factors uniquely through the Jacobian:
$$\forall\,\phi:(C,p)\to A,\ \exists!\ \bar{\phi}:\mathrm{Pic}^{0}_{C/k}\to A\ \text{with}\ \phi=\bar\phi\circ\mathrm{aj}.$$
`AlbaneseUP` (×7): the headline `Pic.albaneseUP` + universal-map descent.

- CECH.acyclic [done/proof/normal] (Cech-Cohomology) Affine acyclicity (Serre vanishing) - **Affine acyclicity (Serre vanishing).**
$$\check{H}^{i}(\mathfrak{U},\mathcal{F})=0\ (i>0)\quad\text{on affine opens},$$
via tilde-vanishing $\Rightarrow$ affine Čech vanishing; localization comparison `AwayComparison`, $\varphi/\varphi_L$ naturality.

- CECH.engine [done/proof/normal] (Cech-Cohomology) Combinatorial / free Čech engine - **Combinatorial / free Čech engine (sorry-free).** Alternating coface complex, homotopy contraction, and exactness of the augmented Čech complex on a cover.

- CECH.main [done/proof/high] (Cech-Cohomology) cech_computes_higherDirectImage (unconditional) - **Comparison theorem `cech_computes_higherDirectImage`.**
$$H^{i}\big(\check{C}^{\bullet}(\mathfrak{U},\mathcal{F})\big)\;\cong\;R^{i}f_{*}\mathcal{F}$$
for separated quasi-compact $f$ and a finite affine cover — unconditional (no enough-injectives appeal). Deliverable merged into AJC.

- CECH.pushpull [done/proof/normal] (Cech-Cohomology) PushPull functoriality - **PushPull functoriality.** `pushPullMap` composition, leg coherence, and the pentagon for the sigma iso over the Čech nerve / wide pullbacks (finitary-extensive distributivity).

- GRQ.cells [done/proof/normal] (GR-Quot-Closure) Grassmannian cells, gluing & descent - **Grassmannian cells, gluing & descent (sorry-free).** Rank-quotient setoid, affine charts, the $\mathrm{GL}_{d}$ transition cocycle, and effective descent gluing the charts into $\mathrm{Grass}(V,d)$.

- GRQ.graded [done/proof/normal] (GR-Quot-Closure) Section graded ring (SNAP) - **Section graded ring (SNAP, sorry-free).** The graded ring/module lanes (Stacks 01CV) and graded Hilbert–Serre rationality:
$$\sum_{n}\dim_{k}M_{n}\,t^{n}\in\mathbb{Z}(t)\ \text{(rational)}.$$

- GRQ.quot [done/proof/normal] (GR-Quot-Closure) Quot scheme (quasi-coherent descent) - **Quot scheme (quasi-coherent descent, sorry-free).** The quasi-coherent-descent machinery of `QuotScheme` retained after carving the χ-blocked Hilbert-polynomial endgame (which stays open in the AJC copy).

- GRQ.represents [done/proof/high] (GR-Quot-Closure) Grassmannian.represents (H^0 leg) - **Representability `Grassmannian.represents` (H^0 leg).**
$$\mathrm{Grass}(V,d)\ \text{represents}\ T\mapsto\{\,V_{T}\twoheadrightarrow Q\ \text{rank }d\,\},\qquad \texttt{tautologicalQuotient\_epi}.$$

- LBC.compinv [done/proof/high] (Line-Bundle-Comparison-Iso) Terminal comparison inverse (Pic^sharp group law) - **Terminal comparison inverse (deliverable — finished).** The keystone `trivialisation_restrict_compat` and the chart-chase inverse, giving the comparison isomorphism that endows $\mathrm{Pic}^{\sharp}_{C/k}$ with its group structure. Merges back into AJC (**T1**).

- LBC.dual [done/proof/normal] (Line-Bundle-Comparison-Iso) Slice-dual transport iso (DUAL route) - **Slice-dual transport iso (DUAL route, sorry-free).**
$$\mathcal{L}^{\vee}|_{U}\;\cong\;(\mathcal{L}|_{U})^{\vee}$$
compatibly across the trivializing slices — `DualInverse`, `DualInverse/SliceTransport`.

- LBC.pullback [done/proof/normal] (Line-Bundle-Comparison-Iso) Line-bundle pullback / relative Pic functor - **Line-bundle pullback / relative Pic functor (sorry-free).** `LineBundlePullback`, `RelPicFunctor`; the seed compatibility $f^{*}(\mathcal L\otimes\mathcal M)\cong f^{*}\mathcal L\otimes f^{*}\mathcal M$ on locally trivial bundles (`pullback_tensor_iso_loctriv`).

- LBC.stalktensor [done/proof/normal] (Line-Bundle-Comparison-Iso) Stalk-tensor / internal-hom machinery - **Stalk-tensor / internal-hom machinery (sorry-free).** `TensorObjSubstrate/StalkTensor`, `PresheafInternalHom` — the local model for $\otimes$ and $\mathcal{H}om$ on quasi-coherent sheaves used by the comparison.

- MR.pic [pending/blueprint/normal] (MR0555258-Compactifying-Picard) Compactifying the Picard scheme (D'Souza) - **Compactifying the Picard scheme** (D'Souza, *MR0555258*). Related-paper formalization: the compactified Jacobian / degenerate Picard variety of a singular curve. Depends on the core AG engine (schemes, cohomology, Picard).

Lean target is a ~3900-line §1 Altman–Kleiman base-change scaffold (`Basic.lean`), now pinned to mathlib v4.31.0 and building green (8560 jobs). One residual `sorry` (`hSupportBC`) plus deliberate `External` axioms remain.

- PIC.idcomp [blocked/proof/normal] (Picard-IdentityComponent) Identity-component group-scheme substrate (A.3 leg) - **Identity-component group-scheme substrate (A.3 leg).** Extraction subproject carrying the `GroupScheme.IdentityComponent`-based construction of $\mathrm{Pic}^{0}_{C/k}$ and its abelian-variety structure:
$$e\in\mathrm{Pic}^{0}_{C/k}=\big(\underline{\mathrm{Pic}}_{C/k}\big)^{\!\circ},\qquad \dim_k\mathrm{Pic}^{0}_{C/k}=g .$$
Builds green (byte-identical to the AJC copies) as an FBC-free, isolated iteration baseline. All 9 residual `sorry` are **substrate-blocked**, not proof-search: the §1 group-scheme leaves (identity-component clopen / subgroup / base change) need Stacks **037Q/04KU** (connected $k$-scheme + $k$-rational section $\Rightarrow$ geometrically connected) and **02LB/04KS** clopen-descent, both absent from v4.31 Mathlib; the $\mathrm{Pic}^{0}$-specific leaves (`Pic0Scheme`, `degree`, `isAbelianVariety`, `finrank_eq_genus`) inherit the FGA sorry-taint (`AJC.picrep`) and the tangent-space comparison (`AJC.pic0av`).

# Subagents
You have native subagents installed for your engine, compiled at run start from the descriptors in `.archon-horizon/subagents/<name>.md` (Claude reads `.claude/agents/`, Codex reads `.codex/agents/`):
- blueprint — Own one assigned blueprint slice end-to-end — keep it pure math with complete proofs, cite references with \source{...} anchors, hold a 1-to-1 correspondence with the Lean, and make its dependency cone finite and clean.
- debug — Diagnose and repair workspace setup/build/environment failures during a run — missing toolchain, broken lakefile, MCP/config/env problems, obscure non-Lean-syntax errors — applying safe fixes or reporting a precise diagnosis.
- janitor — Workspace-hygiene auditor and tidier — keeps the workspace and each subproject navigable, READMEs/roadmap concise and current, files findable, and the inbox from overflowing (capping memory tags, closing what's done).
- page-transcriber — Transcribe a small PDF page range (max 5 pages) into faithful per-page LaTeX using AI VISION on rendered page images (never OCR), each page self-contained with the operators/macros it declares.
- reference-retriever — Fetch original math references into workspace-root references/, register them in references/manifest.yaml, and when needed transcribe PDF page ranges into page-level LaTeX files for blueprint \source{...} anchors.
- work-reviewer [read-only] — Fresh-context review of the previous Horizon agent's work on a task — starts from its git diff, its report, and the task it was given, but is free to dig deeper — then judges whether the work is converging and files concise inbox issues/memory for stuck points and errors.

Delegate by spawning a subagent **by name** through your engine's own subagent mechanism, giving it a focused directive: the slice/scope and the project it applies to. Spawn several in parallel when the work divides cleanly, then wait for and reconcile their reports. Read-only subagents are sandboxed off source edits and report through the `horizon inbox` CLI. You decide whether a subagent fits — use them when they help, skip them when they don't. Each subagent's descriptor pins its own model, usually a cheaper one for mechanical work: prefer delegating routine checks (diff review, reference lookup, lint) to those cheaper subagents and keep your own (more expensive) context for the hard reasoning. To add or change a subagent, edit its descriptor under `.archon-horizon/subagents/` — it recompiles on the next run.

- v4.31.0 migration fix patterns (Cech + GR push-pull/pullback proofs)

Effective repairs for the **v4.31.0 defeq-transparency-wall** breaks in these projects:

- **Port from the AJC copy first.** `MainProjects/Algebraic-Jacobian-Challenge/.../<same file>` is often already sorry-free (e.g. `PresheafCech.lean`, `CechSectionIdentificationBase.lean`). `diff` the subproject vs AJC; if they differ only in the sorry regions, copy the AJC file wholesale.
- **`set_option backward.isDefEq.respectTransparency false in`** before a lemma fixes most "type mismatch under instances transparency" / stuck defeq. **Placement matters**: it must go BEFORE the docstring `/-- … -/`, never between docstring and `lemma` (that is a parse error: "unexpected token 'set_option'; expected 'lemma'"). Stacks with `set_option maxHeartbeats N in`.
- **`erw [Category.comp_id]` / `erw [Category.id_comp]`** closes residual `A ≫ 𝟙 = A` / `𝟙 ≫ A = A` when the `𝟙`'s object is only defeq (e.g. `(𝟭).obj M` vs `M`, or a `⋙`-form) so `simp`/`exact Category.comp_id _` fail. `erw` matches through the wall then auto-`rfl`.
- **`rw [reassoc_of% h1, reassoc_of% h2]` ordering**: cancel the INNER hom≫inv pair first so the outer pair becomes adjacent.
- **`subst h; simp <;> rfl`** for eqToHom-transport lemmas whose two sides reduce to defeq-but-not-syntactic `map = map`.
- **LSP is dead** on these full-`import Mathlib` files (server crashes / broken pipe). Iterate with `lake build <module>` and read the printed unsolved-goal state from the error output. `GlueDescent` ≈ 2.6 min/build; `CechHigherDirectImage` ≈ 8.5 min/build.

- **`CechHigherDirectImage.lean` `rawPushPullMap_comp`**: final transport is pure **term-mode**, no `rw` (avoids "motive not type correct"). Shape: `(congrArg (fun t => (pushforward p₁).map A_head ≫ t) he).trans (((Functor.map_comp _ _ _).symm.trans (congrArg (pushforward p₁).map ((Category.assoc _ _ _).trans INNER))).trans (Functor.map_comp _ _ _))`. `he` rewrites the outer over-triangle `eqToHom` to `(pushforward p₁).map eqInner`; `Category.assoc` fixes `(x≫y)≫z` vs `x≫(y≫z)` before `INNER`; `map_comp` folds/splits.

- **`GlueDescent.lean` `pullback_cast_compat`**: proved from the associativity solved-forms. Recipe: (1) `set_option backward.isDefEq.respectTransparency false in` BEFORE the docstring (the `pullbackComp` solved-forms unfold to the `⋙`-object form; else v4.31 transparency wall blocks matching). (2) tagged `pullbackComp_inv_comp_map_inv_app` + `pullbackComp_inv_comp_congr_hom_app` `@[reassoc]`. (3) phase-1 `simp only [pullbackComp_comp_fst_hom_app, pullback_map_inv_comp_hom_app_assoc, pullback_map_congr_inv_comp_hom_app_assoc, pullbackComp_inv_comp_congr_hom_app_assoc, pullbackComp_inv_comp_map_inv_app(_assoc), Category.assoc]` keeping congr as casts. (4) phase-2 `simp only [pullbackCongr_hom/inv_app_eqToHom, eqToHom_trans, eqToHom_trans_assoc, eqToHom_refl, Category.assoc, comp_id, id_comp]` (need `eqToHom_trans_assoc` for mid-chain). Variance: `pullbackComp x y : pullback y ⋙ pullback x ≅ pullback (x≫y)`.

- Counit of `pullback q ⊣ pushforward q` at open immersion `q` is iso: `IsIso.of_isIso_fac_left (Adjunction.leftAdjointUniq_hom_app_counit (restrictAdjunction q) (pullbackPushforwardAdjunction q) c)` with `haveI` on `(restrictAdjunction q).counit` iso via `inferInstanceAs (IsIso (restrictFunctorAdjCounitIso q).hom)`.
- essImage → unit-iso: `Adjunction.isIso_unit_app_of_iso` (needs `pushforward` Full+Faithful — Mathlib instances for open immersions). Reverse `mem_essImage_of_unit_isIso`.

Poset-hom equalities: `congr 1` after `rw [← Functor.map_comp]` discharges by proof-irrelevance; op-level casts via `Quiver.Hom.unop_inj; Subsingleton.elim`.

- Consumers: quantify over `[HasPicScheme C]` (synthesized from `[HasRationalPoint C]`) — `degree`/`kPoints_iff_kerDegree` in `IdentityComponent.lean` now carry it. `Pic0Scheme C := GroupScheme.IdentityComponent (PicScheme C)` is now a real def (axiom-clean), gated on `PicSchemeLocallyOfFiniteType`.

**Caveat 1 — pointless curves:** `JacobianWitness` (`Jacobian.lean`) requires the abelian variety $J$ to exist UNconditionally, but route (b) only builds `PicScheme` for pointed $C$. For a genus-$\ge 1$ curve without a rational point the eventual `picardJacobianWitness` proof needs Galois/étale descent from a finite extension where $C$ gains a point (or a pointed restatement of the north star). Not blocking anything now — record before anyone tries to close `picardJacobianWitness` directly.

**Caveat 2 — `smoothProperQuotient` statement is WEAKER than Kleiman lm:qt and unprovable as stated:** Mathlib v4.31 has NO quasi-projectivity vocabulary, so hypothesis (2) of lm:qt is missing from the Lean statement; without it the statement is FALSE (Hironaka-type free ℤ/2 action on a smooth proper non-projective 3-fold: smooth proper equivalence relation, étale-quotient sheaf a non-scheme algebraic space). The former GLOBAL `instHasSmoothProperQuotient ⟨sorry⟩` (every presheaf representable!) was deleted this run (Ground-confirmed gone); the class is a use-site hypothesis only. Do NOT reintroduce a global instance, and do NOT try to prove the theorem as stated.
- **Stacks 01BG general pullback-qcoh CLOSED + FBC RHS tilde leaf CLOSED (run 0003 session 0013, T2) — supersedes all "general-morphism pullback qcoh is absent / only in sorry-tainted QuotScheme" notes.**

- `Cohomology/PullbackQuasicoherent.lean` (new, ~185 LOC, axiom-clean): `pullback_isQuasicoherent_hom (g : Y ⟶ X)` for ARBITRARY scheme morphisms. Recipe: localize the pullback, not the presentation — per cover member `A`, `(g^*F)|_{g⁻¹A} ≅ (g.resLE A W)^*(F|_A)` via `pullbackComp`+`pullbackCongr (resLE_comp_ι g le_rfl)`+`restrictFunctorIsoPullback`, transport the global presentation of `F|_A` by `SheafOfModules.Presentation.map` (15-underscore @-application, copy `presentationRestrictSliceOfOver`'s shape), then the CechTermAcyclic over-bridge tail (`modulesOverOpensEquivalence` / `overOpensIsoRestrict` / `overOpensFunctorUnitIso` / counit) and `IsQuasicoherent.of_coversTop` on the preimage cover.
- Enablers now available: `opensMap_final` (`(Opens.map φ).Final` for ANY continuous map — structured-arrow posets are ⊔-directed, `Functor.final_of_isFiltered_structuredArrow`) and `pullbackUnitIso : g^*𝒪_X ≅ 𝒪_Y` (Mathlib's `pullbackObjUnitToUnit` iso under `[F.Final]`). Gotcha: re-synthesis of `IsIso (pullbackObjUnitToUnit …)` at USE sites can fail (defeq wall in the instance-arg unification) — pass the instance explicitly via `@asIso _ _ _ _ _ (pullbackObjUnitToUnit_isIso_hom g)`.
- FBC leaf `pushPullObj_coverInter_baseChanged_pushforward_iso_tilde` (622-line sorry) closed as literally `(pushforward f').mapIso (twisted_cech_nerve_per_sigma …) ≪≫ pushPullObj_coverInter_pushforward_iso_tilde f' 𝒰' (g'^*F) (pullback_isQuasicoherent_hom g' F hF) σ` — the def had to MOVE below `twisted_cech_nerve_per_sigma` in the file. FBC leaves 5 → 4.
- Iteration cost: `lake env lean` on a file importing Mathlib+`CechTermAcyclic` ≈ 4 min under load ~65; the 2-module `lake build` (new file + `CechHigherDirectImageUnconditional`) ≈ 25 min.
- **`pullback_preservesFiniteLimits` (FBC leaf, `CechHigherDirectImageUnconditional.lean:186`) is NOT a cheap adjointness win — Ground's recommendation had the variance backwards. Route analysis (session 0013), do not re-derive.**

- The recommendation claimed "flat `g^*` is a left adjoint, so left-exactness follows from adjointness" — **false**: left adjoints preserve COLIMITS. Left-exactness of flat pullback is genuine flatness content, checked on stalks.
- No dodge exists at the presheaf level: `PresheafOfModules.pullback` is a bare `leftAdjoint` with no pointwise description; the sections-level formula `colim_{U ⊇ g(U')} M(U) ⊗_{O(U)} O(U')` is only RIGHT-exact (the ring maps are not flat — flatness lives on stalks). Sheafification + `SheafOfModules.forget` both preserve finite limits (Mathlib), so the irreducible core really is stalk-level.
- **Viable honest route** (est. 400–800 LOC of new infra): (1) combinators exist — `Functor.preservesFiniteLimits_tfae` / `preservesFiniteLimits_of_preservesKernels` (`Mathlib/Algebra/Homology/ShortComplex/ExactFunctor.lean`); (2) Mathlib 2026 has module structure on stalks (`Mathlib/Algebra/Category/ModuleCat/Stalk.lean`, `PresheafOfModules.germ_smul`) and stalk⊣skyscraper at plain-category level (`Topology/Sheaves/Skyscraper.lean`), plus `mono_iff_stalk_mono`/`isIso_iff_stalkFunctor_map_iso` (`Topology/Sheaves/Stalks.lean`); (3) the missing brick is **stalk-of-pullback**: `stalkModule_x ∘ pullback g ≅ extendScalars(stalkMap) ∘ stalkModule_{g x}` — provable by adjoint uniqueness IF a module-level skyscraper right adjoint is built (pushforward of skyscraper = skyscraper at the image); then flat stalk maps (`AlgebraicGeometry.Flat.iff_flat_stalkMap`-adjacent, `RingHom.Flat` appLE definition) + `ModuleCat.preservesFiniteLimits_extendScalars_of_flat` (`ModuleCat/Descent.lean`) finish via kernel-comparison-on-stalks.
- **Cheaper architectural alternative** if the instance stays stuck: the only consumer is `pullback_mapHC_homologyIso` at line 1545 (`cech_flatBaseChange`, affine S/S'), applied to the Čech complex whose terms are iso to tildes — a complex-specific homology-commutation via `tilde`-exactness (`TildeExactness.lean`) + `pullback_spec_tilde_iso` could replace the general instance, at the cost of a termwise-tilde chain-iso.
- pullback_preservesFiniteLimits: prove via flat extendScalars, NOT adjointness

`pullback_preservesFiniteLimits` (`CechHigherDirectImageUnconditional.lean:193`, instance, `[Flat g]`) is a **true** statement but a genuine open `sorry` — do not attempt a cheap adjointness proof.

- The statement is sound: module pullback $g^*$ = inverse image $g^{-1}$ (exact) followed by $-\otimes_{\mathcal O_S}\mathcal O_{S'}$, which is left-exact **because** `[Flat g]`. Finite limits are preserved.
- The trap: `g^*` is a **left adjoint**, so it preserves colimits / is right-exact — that gives you nothing for finite *limits*. "Left adjoint $\Rightarrow$ left-exact" is backwards. The flatness hypothesis is doing all the work and must be used.
- Intended route (per the leaf's own docstring): transfer along `SheafOfModules.pullbackIso`, discharge `forget`/`sheafification` (already limit-preserving in Mathlib), reduce to `ModuleCat.preservesFiniteLimits_extendScalars_of_flat` stalkwise. Reference Stacks 02KH.
- Note it is a global `instance` carrying a sorry; `pullback_preservesHomology` (line 197) derives from it by `inferInstance`, so the whole flat-homology chain rests on this node.
- v4.31 recipes round 3 (MR0555258 migration): convert descends deeper, Type-hom funext wall, structInst binder-field parse quirk

New patterns from migrating `MR0555258/Basic.lean` v4.30→v4.31 (all verified green, complement the existing v4.31 memories):

- **`convert h using N` descends one level deeper in v4.31**: proofs shaped `convert h using 4; exact congrArg f e` break with a type-mismatch whose 'expected' is the *argument-level* equality. Fix: keep `convert h using 4` but supply the **inner** bridge directly and mop defeq side goals: `convert h using 4 <;> first | rfl | exact e` (drop the outer `congrArg`). Extra side goals are object/category equalities closed by `rfl`.
- **Type-category hom equality: `funext`/`apply funext` does NOT unify** with `f ≫ g = h ≫ i` even under `respectTransparency false`. Mathlib's own idiom (see `FullyFaithful.homNatIso`): `fun f => by ext g; exact hF.map_injective (by simp)`. If simp stalls on `hG.homEquiv.symm` (v4.31 displays via `ConcreteCategory.hom ... .toFun`), run `simp` first, then `show`-restate with `hG.preimage` (defeq to `homEquiv.symm`), then `simp` (fires `map_preimage`).
- **StructInst binder-style field + comma**: `{ app V := e, naturality := ... }` fails to parse ('unexpected identifier; expected }'). Use lambda + newline-separated fields: `{ app := fun V => e\n  naturality := ... }`.
- **`Scheme.Hom.appIso_inv_naturality` exists in v4.31 Mathlib** — exact match for presheaf-map-vs-appIso.inv naturality squares of open immersions; no hand proof needed.
- **`lake update` with a dep pinned to `main` (doc-gen4) silently bumps `lean-toolchain`** (to v4.32.0-rc1 here) and then the mathlib cache refuses to fetch. Pin doc-gen4 to `0bc516c1b9db` (the AJC rev) and rewrite `lean-toolchain` back before re-running.
- MR0555258 iterates at ~110 s per `lake build` of `Basic.lean`; mathlib cache is shared locally (no download needed).
- **A.3 Pic0 cone state after run-0008 T5 — supersedes the run-0005 'IdentityComponent all 9 sorries blocked' memory.**

- **Proved sorry-free**: `Pic0.grpObj` (`Nonempty (GrpObj (Pic0Scheme C))` = `isSubgroupHomomorphism (PicScheme C)`; instances synthesize directly from `groupSchemeStructure` (CommGrpObj parent projection) + the `PicSchemeLocallyOfFiniteType` projection instance; `Pic0Scheme` unfolds by rfl, plain `exact` works). Also `Pic0.geometricallyIrreducible` (specialisation of the substrate), both `isAbelianVariety` assemblies (the `Pic0Scheme`-namespace one MOVED to `Pic0AbelianVariety.lean`), and `tangentSpaceCotangentDual` now hypothesis-free.
- **Still blocked, do not re-probe**: `isFiniteTypeGeometricallyIrreducible` QC∧GeomIrred conjunct (EGA IV₂ 4.5.8/4.6.1 translate-cover argument, not in Mathlib v4.31); `degree`/`kPoints_iff_kerDegree` (Quot/Hilbert χ endgame); `finrank_eq_genus`, `Pic0.smooth`, `Pic0.proper` (need tangentSpaceIso resp. Chevalley–Rosenlicht).
- **tangentSpaceIso attack path opened**: the ring-level truncated-exponential splitting is DONE (`Picard/DualNumberUnits.lean`, axiom-clean: `(R[ε])ˣ ≃* Rˣ × (R,+)` + naturality via `mapRingHom`; blueprint `sec:pic0_trunc_exp`, 15 nodes). Next bricks in order: (1) dual-number points of `Pic0Scheme` ↔ `ker(picSharp(Spec k[ε]) → picSharp(Spec k))` via `(representable C).homEquiv` (clopen ⟹ Pic vs Pic⁰ dual-number points at 0 agree); (2) sheaf-level sequence on `C_ε = C ×ₖ Spec k[ε]` (identify `O_{C_ε}` sections with `O_C(U)[ε]`); (3) Čech `H¹(O^×)` cocycle classification of line bundles trivial mod ε.
- **FBC naturality leaves: precise missing layer (post-heart-closure, run 0009 T2; supersedes '4 leaf sorries' notes).** FBC has exactly **3 sorries**, all in `CechHigherDirectImageUnconditional.lean`: the two cosimplicial `naturality` fields (`cech_pushforward_baseChange_natIso` ~1690, `twisted_cech_nerve_iso` ~1758) and `pullback_preservesFiniteLimits` (197). `FlatBaseChange.lean` is sorry-free (mate stubs deleted).

- **The naturality fields are NOT quick wins.** Both components are ≪≫-chains through `pushPull_sigma_iso` + `Pi.mapIso` of per-σ isos. Missing layer, in order: (1) a transport formula expressing `(CechNerve 𝒰 F).map φ` through `pushPull_sigma_iso` as a matrix of restrictions `pushPullObj F (Over.mk j_σ) ⟶ pushPullObj F (Over.mk j_τ)` (only `pushPull_sigma_iso_π`/`_π_incl` exist, in `CechSectionIdentificationBase/Leg`); (2) per-σ restriction compatibility of the heavy per-σ isos (`pushPullObj_coverInter_baseChange`, `twisted_cech_nerve_per_sigma`) — coherence through the `AffinePushPullEssImage` essImage/adjoint constructions. Estimate: multi-hundred LOC each; one focused session per leaf at best.
- **Heart-closure recipe that landed** (session 0011 surgery, verified 0013): paste `restrictedCartesianAffinePushout` onto the ambient square (`IsPullback.paste_vert`), convert with the converse `isPushout_of_isPullback_SpecMap` (`IsPullback.of_map_of_faithful` on `Scheme.Spec`, then `.unop.flip`), identify the pullback corner with `coverInterOpen 𝒰' σ` via the slice iso, rewrite corner sections (`coverInterOpen_baseChange_restrictedMap_comm` + `pullbackRestrict_iso_tilde` + `pullback_spec_tilde_iso`), finish with `affinePushforwardPullbackBaseChange`. `set_option maxHeartbeats 800000` scoped per declaration suffices.
- **Ops (run 0009 session 0013): idle-box build speed, `horizon commit` gotchas, zombie sessions.**

- **Build costs are LOAD artifacts, not constants.** At load ~0.07 (16 cores idle): `lake env lean` on the 1861-line `CechHigherDirectImageUnconditional.lean` = **33 s**; incremental full `lake build` (8642 jobs) ≈ 1 min. The 'hours per build' memories (LegMid1 5h40m etc.) were load-65 measurements. Check `uptime` before budgeting builds.
- **`horizon commit` fails with an EMPTY error** in two cases: (a) no git identity — export `GIT_COMMITTER_NAME='Archon Horizon' GIT_COMMITTER_EMAIL='archon-horizon@local'` (+ AUTHOR pair) first; (b) nothing to commit — the workspace ledger's end-of-run sweep (`workspace[NNNN] run finished`) may have already integrated files you think are stranded. Diagnose with `git --git-dir=.archon-horizon/vcs/workspace.git --work-tree=. diff HEAD --stat -- <project-path>`. Per-project journals under `.archon-horizon/vcs/<project>.git` can lag several runs behind — that's the orchestrator's job.
- **Zombie Horizon sessions are real**: the harness can reconcile and launch a successor while the previous session's `claude` process (reparented to init, transcript flush frozen) is STILL editing files and launching builds in the same project. At session start check `pgrep -af claude` + parent PIDs; terminate a same-project claude whose horizon runner is dead, but read its transcript tail first and inherit its pending intent (session 0011's uncommitted surgery was sound and became the session's main result).

# Recommendation for the next agent (write `recommendation.md`)
A few short bullets for the next Ground/Horizon session — **suggestions grounded in what you saw, not orders**. The next agent self-scopes and may know better. Lead each bullet with the reason, then the suggestion, e.g. `- The cohomology vanishing lemma is the last gap before X, so it looks like the most valuable next target.` Keep it concise; skip status recaps. Human-facing notes go to an `info` inbox item, durable dead ends to memory.

This is a ONE-SHOT headless session: when you produce your final report the session ENDS and you will NOT be re-invoked. Nothing calls you back when a background job finishes, and any background process you started is killed when the session exits. So you cannot 'kick off a build and wait to be notified' — that notification never comes here, and the build dies.
Therefore: if a build, subagent, monitor, or shell command produces a result your conclusion depends on, you MUST get that result WITHIN this session — run it in the FOREGROUND and block on it (read its exit code), or actively poll until it has definitively finished, before writing the report. A heavy `lake build` can take many minutes; budget for that and wait it out. If it genuinely cannot finish in time, do NOT imply success: record the unfinished build under `## Issues`, state what's still unknown, and leave the explicit next action — never write a conclusion that assumes a pending result.

Finish with a brief run-local report. Recommended sections: `## Summary`, `## Progress`, `## Issues`, and `## Next`; add or rename sections if another shape is clearer. Prefer short bullets, and keep each bullet around 20 words or fewer. Always mention bugs, build failures, broken proofs, suspicious code issues, blocked dependencies, unresolved assumptions, and checks that failed or were not run. For this Ground session, make the report complete enough that a human or Ground can understand the session before opening raw logs.

1	/-
2	Copyright (c) 2026 Christian Merten. All rights reserved.
3	Released under Apache 2.0 license as described in the file LICENSE.
4	Authors: Christian Merten
5	-/
6	import Mathlib
7	
8	/-!
9	# Flattening stratification of a coherent sheaf (A.2.a)
10	
11	This file is the **A.2.a** file-skeleton sub-build chapter for the project's
12	positive-genus arm of `nonempty_jacobianWitness`. It packages the
13	Nitsure~\S4 flattening-stratification theorem — the technical heart that
14	turns the absolute Quot-functor representability problem on a projective
15	morphism `π : X ⟶ S` with coherent input `𝓕` into a finite collection of
16	locally-closed strata over which `𝓕` becomes flat.
17	
18	## Status (iter-176 Lane E file-skeleton — re-dispatch)
19	
20	iter-175 Lane E died to the Anthropic session-limit reset window without
21	ever calling `Write` (the file was never created). iter-176 re-dispatches
22	the file-skeleton verbatim. Each blueprint-pinned declaration carries the
23	*intended* substantive type signature (matching the `\lean{...}` pin in
24	`blueprint/src/chapters/Picard_FlatteningStratification.tex`) with a
25	`sorry` body. The bodies are iter-177+ work; the substantive proofs are
26	deep (Nitsure~\S4 ~250-line argument: generic-flatness ⟶ Noetherian
27	induction ⟶ Castelnuovo–Mumford regularity ⟶ assembly via direct images
28	`E_i = π_* 𝓕(N+i)`).
29	
30	The 4 blueprint-pinned declarations are:
31	
32	1. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.CoherentSheafFlat` (def, ~8 LOC) — the
33	   **coherent-sheaf flatness predicate** for a coherent `𝓕` on `X` over
34	   `S` via `f : X ⟶ S`. Affine-locally: for `U ⊆ S` and `V ⊆ f⁻¹U` both
35	   affine, `Γ(𝓕, V)` is a flat `Γ(S, U)`-module via the composite
36	   `Γ(S, U) → Γ(X, V) → End(Γ(𝓕, V))`. The corresponding Stacks tag is
37	   00HB (module-level) lifted to a sheaf-level predicate by the
38	   affine-local descent characterisation.
39	
40	2. `AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatness` (theorem, ~15 LOC) — **generic
41	   flatness** [Nitsure §4 Theorem]: over a noetherian integral base
42	   `S`, a coherent sheaf on a finite-type `X ⟶ S` is flat over a
43	   non-empty open `V ⊆ S`.
44	
45	3. `AlgebraicGeometry.flatteningStratification` (theorem, ~25 LOC) — the
46	   **flattening-stratification existence theorem** [Nitsure §4]: for `S`
47	   noetherian and `𝓕` coherent on a proper `π : X ⟶ S`, there exists a
48	   finite locally-closed stratification `{S_f}` of `S` (set-theoretically
49	   covering, pairwise disjoint) such that `𝓕` becomes flat on each
50	   stratum.
51	
52	4. `AlgebraicGeometry.flatteningStratification_universal` (theorem, ~15 LOC)
53	   — the **universal property** of the stratification [Nitsure §4 (ii)]:
54	   any morphism `φ : T ⟶ S` such that `𝓕` pulled back to `X ×_S T` is
55	   `T`-flat factors uniquely through the stratum coproduct
56	   `i : ⨿ S_f ⟶ S`.
57	
58	Additionally, three sub-lemmas decompose the existence proof, plus a
59	curve-specialisation corollary feeding the Route~A consumer:
60	
61	5. `AlgebraicGeometry.flatLocusStratification` (lemma, ~12 LOC) — the
62	   **special case `n = 0`** [Nitsure §4, n=0]: a coherent `𝓕` on `S`
63	   admits a finite locally-closed stratification by integer "rank"
64	   strata.
65	
66	6. `AlgebraicGeometry.flatLocusReduction` (lemma, ~12 LOC) — the
67	   **Noetherian-induction reduction** [Nitsure §4 general case opening]:
68	   a finite reduced locally-closed stratification on which `𝓕`
69	   *already* becomes flat (without polynomial indexing).
70	
71	7. `AlgebraicGeometry.flatLocusAssembly` (lemma, ~15 LOC) — the
72	   **assembly step** [Nitsure §4 via direct images]: combining the
73	   `n = 0` stratification iteratively on the direct images
74	   `E_i = π_* 𝓕(N+i)` to refine to the Hilbert-polynomial-indexed
75	   stratification.
76	
77	8. `AlgebraicGeometry.flatteningStratification.ofCurve` (theorem, ~10 LOC)
78	   — the **relative-curve specialisation** for the Route~A consumer
79	   `C ×_k T → T`.
80	
81	## Note on type expressivity
82	
83	Following the project rule "Never weaken the type to dodge the proof",
84	each declaration carries a substantive, non-tautological type:
85	
86	- `CoherentSheafFlat` reduces to module-theoretic flatness of every
87	  affine-local section module over the corresponding affine-base ring
88	  via the `appLE` ring-hom — non-empty content because flatness of a
89	  module is the standard non-trivial homological condition.
90	- `genericFlatness` requires the existence of a *non-empty* open `V`
91	  and substantive flatness on every affine `U ⊆ V`; both quantifiers
92	  are necessary (without them the statement collapses to `V = ∅`).
93	- `flatteningStratification` packages a *finite* indexed family of
94	  *locally-closed-immersions* `ι : S_f ⟶ S` that pairwise *cover*
95	  `|S|` *disjointly* AND on which the pullback `𝓕|_{X ×_S S_f}` becomes
96	  `S_f`-flat — all four conjuncts are substantive (any single
97	  one trivialises if dropped).
98	- `flatteningStratification_universal` packages the universal property
99	  via `∃!` (unique existence of factorisation), a substantive Yoneda
100	  bijection content.
101	
102	## Mathlib status
103	
104	Mathlib (master `b80f227`) provides `Module.Flat`,
105	`AlgebraicGeometry.Flat` (morphism-level), `IsImmersion`,
106	`IsLocallyNoetherian`, `IsIntegral`, `LocallyOfFiniteType`. It does NOT
107	provide: a relative projective space `ℙⁿ_S`, an `IsProjective` morphism
108	property, the flattening-stratification construction, or coherent-sheaf
109	flatness as a sheaf-level predicate.
110	
111	The current file-skeleton uses `IsProper π` as the structural stand-in
112	for "projective `π`" (every projective morphism is proper; the
113	restriction is harmless in the Route~A consumer setting where `π` comes
114	from a smooth proper curve, which is automatically projective).
115	iter-177+ refinement: once Mathlib gains an `IsProjective` morphism
116	property, the hypothesis tightens.
117	
118	## References
119	
120	Blueprint: `blueprint/src/chapters/Picard_FlatteningStratification.tex`

=== File header (status) ===
/-

Released under Apache 2.0 license as described in the file LICENSE.
Authors: Christian Merten
-/
import Mathlib

/-!
# The Quot scheme (A.2.b)

This file is the **A.2.b** file-skeleton sub-build chapter for the project's
positive-genus arm of `nonempty_jacobianWitness`. It packages the
Grothendieck–Altman–Kleiman Quot-scheme construction
`Quot^{Φ,L}_{E/X/S}` — a projective `S`-scheme representing the functor of
`T`-flat coherent quotients of `E_T` on `X_T = X ×_S T` with Hilbert
polynomial `Φ` on every fiber — together with the in-project sub-build for
the Grassmannian *scheme* (Mathlib at the pinned commit carries only a
linear-algebra Grassmannian).

## Status (iter-176 Lane H file-skeleton — re-dispatch)

iter-175 Lane H died to the Anthropic session-limit reset window without
ever calling `Write` (the file was never created). iter-176 re-dispatches
the file-skeleton verbatim. Each of the six blueprint-pinned declarations
carries the *intended* substantive type signature (matching the
`\lean{...}` pin in `blueprint/src/chapters/Picard_QuotScheme.tex`) with a
`sorry` body. The bodies are iter-177+ work; the substantive proofs are
deep (Nitsure §5: boundedness ⟶ Grassmannian embedding ⟶ flattening
stratification ⟶ valuative criterion).

The 6 blueprint-pinned declarations are:

1. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.hilbertPolynomial` (def, ~5 LOC) — the
   **Hilbert polynomial function** `s ↦ Φ_{F,s} ∈ ℚ[λ]` of a coherent
   sheaf `F` on `X` over a finite-type `π : X ⟶ S` with respect to a
   line bundle `L`. Encoded as a function `S → Polynomial ℚ`.

2. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QuotFunctor` (def, ~6 LOC) — the **Quot
   functor** `Quot^{Φ,L}_{E/X/S} : (Sch/S)^op ⥤ Set` sending an
   `S`-scheme `T ⟶ S` to the set of equivalence classes
   `⟨F, q⟩` of pairs `(F, q)` with `F` a `T`-flat coherent sheaf on
   `X_T`, `q : E_T ↠ F` a surjection, and `F|_{X_t}` having Hilbert
   polynomial `Φ` at every `t ∈ T`.

3. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Grassmannian` (def, ~5 LOC) — the
   **Grassmannian functor** `Grass(V, d) : (Sch/S)^op ⥤ Set` of
   rank-`d` quotients of a locally free `O_S`-module `V`.

4. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Grassmannian.representable` (theorem, ~8 LOC)
   — the **representability of the Grassmannian** by a smooth projective
   `S`-scheme `Gr_S(V, d)` of relative dimension `d(r-d)`, equipped with
   the Plücker closed embedding into `ℙ_S(⋀^d V)`.

5. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QuotScheme` (theorem, ~10 LOC) — the
   **Grothendieck–Altman–Kleiman representability theorem** for the Quot
   functor: for a noetherian `S`, a projective `π : X ⟶ S`, a relatively
   very ample `L` on `X`, a coherent `E`, and `Φ ∈ ℚ[λ]`, the functor
   `Quot^{Φ,L}_{E/X/S}` is representable by a projective `S`-scheme.

6. `AlgebraicGeometry.flatBaseChangeCohomology` (theorem, ~10 LOC) — the

<system-reminder>[Truncated: PARTIAL view — showing lines 1-852 of 1169 total (29154 tokens, cap 25000). Call Read with offset=853 limit=852 for the next page, or Grep to find a specific section. Do NOT answer from this page alone if the answer may be further in the file.]</system-reminder>

1	\chapter{FGA representability of the Picard scheme}
2	\label{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FGAPicRepresentability.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-174). This chapter is the A.2.c assembly sub-row of
6	% Route~A (positive-genus arm of nonempty_jacobianWitness). It wires the
7	% Quot-scheme engine (\cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}, A.2.b) and the
8	% \'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor (\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor},
9	% A.1.c) into the FGA representability statement for \(\Pic_{C/k}\), following
10	% Kleiman, ``The Picard scheme'' \S 4 (\cite{kleiman-picard}). It is a small
11	% \emph{assembly} chapter: every nontrivial input is a \(\) pointer to a
12	% sibling chapter, and the work here is to thread the inputs through
13	% Grothendieck's existence theorem (\cite[\S 4, Main Theorem
14	% th:main]{kleiman-picard}) and its specialisation to a complete variety over a
15	% field (\cite[\S 4, Cor.~cor:algsch]{kleiman-picard}). Identity-component
16	% material (\Picz) is A.3 and lives in a separate sibling chapter --- it is
17	% \emph{not} scoped into this chapter.
18	
19	% NOTE (planner directive iter-174). This chapter must produce the
20	% \emph{representable Picard scheme} \(\Pic_{C/k}\) as a \(k\)-group scheme,
21	% locally of finite type, that represents the \'etale sheafification
22	% \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) defined in A.1.c. The three deliverables are
23	% (i) a line-bundle / Quot translation expressed via the Abel map
24	% \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \to \Pic_{C/k}\) (Kleiman \S 3, \S 4) which factorises through the
25	% Hilbert scheme \(\Hilb_{C/k} = \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\), hence through the
26	% Quot engine of A.2.b; (ii) the existence theorem itself, in the
27	% field-of-definition specialisation \(S = \Spec k\), \(X = C\) a smooth proper
28	% geometrically integral curve; (iii) the group-scheme refinement carried by
29	% the tensor product of invertible sheaves on \(C \times_k T\). The Lean
30	% encoding is delegated to a Lean file
31	% \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FGAPicRepresentability.lean} whose
32	% construction is described in the final section but not written in this
33	% chapter.
34	
35	\section{Setup and motivation}
36	\label{sec:fga_pic_setup}
37	
38	Throughout this chapter, fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically
39	integral curve \(C\) over \(k\) (the curve carrying the Jacobian). The relative
40	Picard presheaf \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \Set\) of
41	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback} has already been packaged --- as a
42	set-valued functor at A.1.b, and refined to its abelian-group-valued
43	\'etale-sheafified version \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) at
44	\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} (A.1.c). The Quot scheme machinery of
45	\cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme} (A.2.b) supplies the representing engine: for
46	\(C/k\) projective and flat with integral geometric fibers, the Hilbert scheme
47	\(\Hilb_{C/k}\) and its open subscheme \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\) of relative effective
48	divisors are both representable by quasi-projective \(k\)-schemes. Grothendieck's
49	existence theorem for \(\Pic\) assembles these two ingredients via the Abel
50	map \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) to produce a representing scheme,
51	the \emph{Picard scheme} \(\Pic_{C/k}\). The present chapter packages this
52	assembly: a line-bundle / Quot correspondence, the FGA representability
53	theorem, and the abelian-group-scheme refinement.
54	
55	\medskip
56	
57	\textbf{The sheafification route (run-0008 rewire).} Kleiman \S 4 represents
58	the \'etale-sheafified functor \(\Pic_{(C/k)\et}\). This chapter instead
59	works throughout under the standing hypothesis that \emph{\(C\) has a
60	\(k\)-rational point} (\cref{def:has_rational_point}) and states
61	representability against the \emph{plain} relative Picard functor
62	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) of \cref{def:rel_pic_sharp}. This is justified by
63	Kleiman \S 2, Thm.~2.5 (\texttt{th:comp}): if \(f : X \to S\) has a section
64	and \(\mathcal O_{S'} = f'_* \mathcal O_{X'}\) holds for all base changes
65	(automatic for our proper geometrically integral curve, by cohomology and
66	base change), then the comparison maps
67	\[
68	  \Pic_{X/S}(T) \longrightarrow \Pic_{(X/S)\et}(T) \longrightarrow
69	  \Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}(T)
70	\]
71	are bijective for every \(T\). Hence under the rational-point hypothesis the
72	plain relative functor \emph{is} the \'etale sheaf, and no \'etale
73	Grothendieck topology (absent from Mathlib at the pinned revision) is
74	needed. Without a section the plain functor is not even a Zariski sheaf in
75	general, so every representability assertion of this chapter carries the
76	rational-point hypothesis; the Jacobian consumers downstream
77	(\cref{chap:AbelJacobi}) work with a pointed curve \((C, P)\), which
78	supplies it.
79	
80	% SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.4);
81	% arXiv:math/0504020, p.~26. Read from
82	% references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex.
83	
84	\section{Line bundles and the Quot / Hilbert scheme}
85	\label{sec:fga_pic_line_bundle_quot}
86	
87	The bridge from line bundles on \(C \times_k T\) to the Quot scheme is the
88	Abel map: a relative effective divisor \(D \subset C \times_k T\) over \(T\)
89	(an \(S\)-flat closed subscheme whose ideal is invertible) determines a closed
90	subscheme of \(C \times_k T\) flat over \(T\), hence a \(T\)-point of the Hilbert
91	functor \(\Hilb_{C/k}(T)\), which is the special case \(\Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\)
92	of the Quot functor of \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}; and it determines an
93	invertible sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}(D)\) on the relative curve,
94	representing a class in \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\).
95	
96	\begin{lemma}\leanok
97	[Line bundle / Quot correspondence via the Abel map]
98	  \label{lem:line_bundle_quot_correspondence}
99	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.abelMap}
100	  \uses{thm:quot_representable, thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined,
101	        def:rel_pic_sharp, def:has_abel_map,
102	        def:inst_has_abel_map,
103	        def:div_functor_carrier, def:pic_sharp_carrier}
104	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 3, Def.~dfn:Abel (the Abel
105	  % map), and Thm.~th:repDiv (representability of relative effective
106	  % divisors by an open subscheme of \(\IHilb_{X/S}\)); arXiv:math/0504020,
107	  % pp.~25, 23 (read from
108	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1837-L1841 + L2135-L2145).
109	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
110	  % "\begin{thm}\label{th:repDiv}
111	  %   Assume \(X/S\) is projective and flat.  Then \(\mathrm{Div}_{X/S}\) is
112	  %  representable by an open subscheme \(\IDiv_{X/S}\) of the Hilbert scheme
113	  %  \(\IHilb_{X/S}\).
114	  %   \end{thm}"
115	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
116	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{dfn:Abel}
117	  %   The {\it Abel map\/} is the natural map of functors
118	  %      \(\)A_{X/S}(T)\:\mathrm{Div}_{X/S}(T)\to \Pic_{X/S}(T)\(\)
119	  %   defined by sending a relative effective divisor \(D\) on \(X_T/T\) to the
120	  %  sheaf \(\mc O_{\!X_T}(D)\).  The target \(\Pic_{X/S}\) may be replaced by any
121	  %  of its associated sheaves."
122	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 3,
123	  Thm.~3.7 + Def.~4.6 (cf.\ [Nitsure], ``Construction of Hilbert
124	  and Quot Schemes'', \S 1, where \(\Hilb_{X/S} = \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_X/X/S}\)).}
125	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve, and let
126	  \(T\) be a \(k\)-scheme. There is a natural map of presheaves on \((\Sch/k)^{op}\)
127	  \[
128	    A_{C/k} \;\colon\; \mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \;\longrightarrow\; \Pic^\sharp_{C/k},
129	    \qquad D \;\longmapsto\; [\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}(D)],
130	  \]
131	  where \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}(T)\) is the set of relative effective Cartier divisors
132	  on \(C \times_k T\) flat over \(T\), and the target is the relative Picard
133	  functor of \cref{def:rel_pic_sharp} (per Kleiman \S 3, Def.~dfn:Abel, ``the
134	  target \(\Pic_{X/S}\) may be replaced by any of its associated sheaves'';
135	  under the chapter's standing rational-point hypothesis the plain relative
136	  functor already coincides with its \'etale sheafification, see
137	  \cref{sec:fga_pic_setup}). The functor \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\) is representable
138	  by an open subscheme \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \subseteq \Hilb_{C/k}\) of the Hilbert scheme
139	  of \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme} (with \(\Hilb_{C/k}\) the special case
140	  \(\Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\) of the Quot functor). The Abel map is therefore
141	  representable by a morphism of \(k\)-schemes \(A_{C/k} : \mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \to
142	  \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\).
143	\end{lemma}
144	
145	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex,
146	% L1843-L1866, proof of th:repDiv)
147	% "Set \(H:=\IHilb_{X/S}\), and let \(W\subset X\x H\) be the universal
148	% (closed) subscheme, and \(q\:W\to H\) the projection.  Let \(V\) denote the
149	% set of points \(w\in W\) at which \(W\) is an effective divisor. Plainly \(V\)
150	% is open in \(W\).  Set \(Z:=q(W-V)\).  Then \(Z\) is closed because \(q\) is
151	% proper.  Set \(U:=H-Z\).  Then \(U\) is open, and \(q^{-1}U\) is an effective
152	% divisor in \(X\x U\).  In fact, since \(q\) is flat, \(q^{-1}U\) is a relative
153	% effective divisor in \(X\x U/U\).
154	% [\dots]"
155	\begin{proof}
156	  
157	  By \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}, the Hilbert functor \(\Hilb_{C/k} =
158	  \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\) is representable by a \(k\)-scheme; this uses
159	  \(C/k\) projective and flat (the smooth proper curve hypothesis is
160	  stronger). Let \(W \subseteq C \times_k \Hilb_{C/k}\) be the universal
161	  closed subscheme and \(q : W \to \Hilb_{C/k}\) the projection. The locus
162	  \(V \subseteq W\) where \(W\) is locally an effective Cartier divisor (i.e.\
163	  cut out by one regular element) is open. The complement \(W \setminus V\)
164	  is closed in \(W\), and the image \(Z := q(W \setminus V) \subseteq
165	  \Hilb_{C/k}\) is closed because \(q\) is proper. Hence \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k} :=
166	  \Hilb_{C/k} \setminus Z\) is open in \(\Hilb_{C/k}\) and represents the
167	  subfunctor of relative effective divisors: on \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\) the
168	  restriction of \(W\) is flat and cut out by one regular element fiberwise,
169	  so by the standard relative-divisor criterion (Kleiman~\S 3,
170	  Lem.~3.4) it is a relative effective divisor on \(C \times_k
171	  \mathrm{Div}_{C/k} / \mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\).
172	
173	  The Abel map sends a relative effective divisor \(D \subseteq C \times_k T\)
174	  to the invertible sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}(D) := \mathcal{I}_D^{-1}\)
175	  (the dual of the ideal sheaf, which is invertible by the divisor
176	  hypothesis). The assignment is functorial in \(T\): pullback of an ideal
177	  sheaf along \(\mathrm{id}_C \times_k g : C \times_k T' \to C \times_k T\)
178	  takes the ideal of \(D\) to the ideal of \(D_{T'}\) (since \(D\) is flat over
179	  \(T\), the schematic inverse image \(D_{T'}\) is a relative effective
180	  divisor on \(C \times_k T'/T'\), again by Kleiman~\S 3, Lem.~3.4), hence
181	  takes \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}(D)\) to \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T'}(D_{T'})\).
182	  Composing with the canonical projection
183	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) of
184	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} yields the
185	  natural map \(A_{C/k}(T) : \mathrm{Div}_{C/k}(T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\).
186	\end{proof}
187	
188	\section{The FGA representability theorem}
189	\label{sec:fga_pic_representability}
190	
191	We now state and outline the proof of Grothendieck's existence theorem
192	for the Picard scheme, in the specialisation needed for the Jacobian arm
193	of Route~A: \(S = \Spec k\), \(X = C\) a smooth proper geometrically integral
194	curve. The general theorem (Kleiman \S 4, Thm.~4.8) covers any
195	\(X/S\) projective Zariski-locally over \(S\), flat, with integral geometric
196	fibers; we record the general statement and then specialise.
197	
198	\begin{theorem}\leanok
199	[FGA representability of the Picard scheme]
200	  \label{thm:fga_pic_representability}
201	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.representable}
202	  \uses{lem:line_bundle_quot_correspondence, lem:smooth_proper_quotient,
203	        thm:quot_representable, def:rel_pic_sharp, def:has_rational_point,
204	        cor:flattening_stratification_curve, def:pic_sharp_representable,
205	        def:inst_pic_sharp_representable, lem:cech_flat_base_change}
206	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4, Main Thm.~th:main,
207	  % and Cor.~cor:algsch (specialisation to \(S = \Spec k\), \(X\) complete);
208	  % arXiv:math/0504020, pp.~26, 35 (read from
209	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2155-L2166 + L2686-L2690).
210	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
211	  % "\begin{thm}[Main]\label{th:main}
212	  %   Assume \(f\:X\to S\) is projective Zariski locally over \(S\), and is flat
213	  %  with integral geometric fibers.
214	  %
215	  %  \tu{(1)} Then \(\IPic_{X/S}\) exists, is separated and locally of finite
216	  %  type over \(S\), and represents \(\Pic_{(X/S)\et}\).
217	  %
218	  %  \tu{(2)} If also \(S\) is Noetherian and \(X/S\) is projective, then
219	  %  \(\IPic_{X/S}\) is a disjoint union of open
220	  %   subschemes, each an increasing union of open quasi-projective
221	  %  \(S\)-schemes.
222	  %   \end{thm}"
223	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
224	  % "\begin{sbscor}\label{cor:algsch}
225	  %    Assume \(S\) is the spectrum of a field \(k\), and \(X\) is complete.  Then
226	  %  \(\IPic_{X/k}\) exists, represents \(\Pic_{(X/k)\fppf}\), and is a disjoint
227	  %  union of open quasi-projective subschemes.
228	  %   \end{sbscor}"
229	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4, Main Thm.~4.8
230	  + Cor.~4.18.3.}
231	  Let \(k\) be a field and \(C\) a smooth proper geometrically integral curve
232	  over \(k\) \emph{with a \(k\)-rational point}
233	  (\cref{def:has_rational_point}). Then the relative Picard functor
234	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) of \cref{def:rel_pic_sharp} --- which under the
235	  rational-point hypothesis coincides with its \'etale sheafification
236	  \(\Pic_{(C/k)\et}\), by Kleiman \S 2, Thm.~2.5 --- is representable by a
237	  \(k\)-scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\), separated and locally of finite type over
238	  \(k\), which is a disjoint union of open quasi-projective
239	  \(k\)-subschemes, each an increasing union of open quasi-projective
240	  subschemes. The scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\) is called the \emph{Picard scheme}
241	  of \(C/k\).
242	\end{theorem}
243	
244	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex,
245	% L2168-L2366, proof of th:main; long and is broken into the four-step
246	% sketch below per the project's structural restatement convention. The
247	% verbatim quote of the opening reduction step is:)
248	% "By~\cite[(0, 4.5.5), p.~106]{EGAG}, it is a local matter on \(S\) to
249	% represent a Zariski sheaf on the category of \(S\)-schemes.  Moreover, it
250	% is also a local matter on \(S\) to prove that an \(S\)-scheme is separated
251	% and locally of finite type.  Hence, in order to prove (1), we may assume
252	% \(S\) is Noetherian and \(X/S\) is projective.
253	% [\dots]
254	% Plainly an \(S\)-scheme is separated if it is a disjoint union of
255	% separated open subschemes, or if it is an increasing union of
256	% separated open subschemes.  Hence (1) follows from (2)."
257	% (subsequent steps quoted inline in the project-notation proof below).
258	\begin{proof}
259	  
260	  We follow Kleiman~\S 4, Thm.~4.8, specialised to \(S = \Spec k\) and
261	  \(X = C\). The proof proceeds in four conceptual steps, preceded by a
262	  comparison step; the underlying algebraic engine is the Quot scheme of
263	  \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme} and the relative Picard functor of
264	  \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}, together with the line-bundle / Quot
265	  correspondence of \cref{lem:line_bundle_quot_correspondence}.
266	
267	  \emph{Step 0: reduce to the \'etale sheaf via the rational point.}
268	  By Kleiman \S 2, Thm.~2.5, since \(C \to \Spec k\) has a section
269	  (\cref{def:has_rational_point}) and \(\mathcal O_T = \pi_{T*}
270	  \mathcal O_{C \times_k T}\) holds for every \(k\)-scheme \(T\) (cohomology
271	  and base change for the proper geometrically integral fibers of
272	  \(C\times_k T/T\)), the comparison map
273	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \to \Pic_{(C/k)\et}(T)\) is bijective for all
274	  \(T\). It therefore suffices to represent the \'etale sheaf
275	  \(\Pic_{(C/k)\et}\), which is what Kleiman's theorem produces; the same
276	  scheme then represents \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\).
277	
278	  \emph{Step 1: stratify by Hilbert polynomial.}
279	  For each polynomial \(\phi \in \mathbb{Q}[n]\), let \(P^\phi \subseteq
280	  \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) be the \'etale subsheaf whose \(T\)-points are
281	  represented by invertible sheaves \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(C \times_k T\) with
282	  \(\chi(C_t, \mathcal{L}_t^{-1}(n)) = \phi(n)\) for all \(t \in T\) (the
283	  Hilbert polynomial relative to the chosen polarisation
284	  \(\mathcal{O}_C(1)\) on the projective curve \(C\)). The \(P^\phi\) form a
285	  disjoint open cover of \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) (openness via Kleiman's
286	  flat-base-change argument, \cref{lem:cech_flat_base_change} / Stacks Tag 02KH;
287	  cf.\ EGA III 7.9.11). It suffices to
288	  represent each \(P^\phi\) by an increasing union of open quasi-projective
289	  \(k\)-schemes.
290	
291	  \emph{Step 2: bound twists by \(m\)-regularity.}
292	  Given \(\phi\), fix \(m\) large enough that the \(T\)-points of \(P^\phi\) are
293	  representable by \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(C \times_k T\) satisfying
294	  \(R^i \pi_{T*} \mathcal{L}(n) = 0\) for all \(i \ge 1\), \(n \ge m\). Twisting
295	  by \(\mathcal{O}_C(m)\) defines an automorphism of \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\)
296	  that carries \(P^\phi\) to \(P^{\phi_0}_0\) for \(\phi_0(n) := \phi(m + n)\).
297	  Hence it suffices to represent each \(P^{\phi_0}_0\) by a quasi-projective
298	  \(k\)-scheme.
299	
300	  \emph{Step 3: factor through the Abel map.}
301	  Consider the Abel map \(A_{C/k} : \mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\)
302	  of \cref{lem:line_bundle_quot_correspondence}. The source \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\)
303	  is representable by an open subscheme of \(\Hilb_{C/k}\) (and hence by an
304	  open subscheme of the Quot scheme of \cref{thm:quot_representable}), and
305	  is quasi-projective over \(k\). Pulling back along
306	  \(P^{\phi_0}_0 \hookrightarrow \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) yields an open
307	  subscheme \(Z := P^{\phi_0}_0 \times_{\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}} \mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\)
308	  of \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\), which is again quasi-projective. The projection
309	  \(\alpha : Z \to P^{\phi_0}_0\) is a surjection of \'etale sheaves:
310	  given a \(T\)-point \(\lambda\) of \(P^{\phi_0}_0\) represented by an \'etale
311	  cover \(T' \to T\) and an invertible sheaf \(\mathcal{L}'\) on \(C \times_k
312	  T'\) with \(H^1(C_{t'}, \mathcal{L}'_{t'}) = 0\), the projective bundle
313	  \(\mathbb{P}(\mathcal{Q}) \to T'\) associated to
314	  \(\mathcal{Q} := \pi_{T'*}\mathcal{L}'\) is smooth over \(T'\); an \'etale
315	  cover \(T_1 \to T'\) admits a \(T'\)-map \(T_1 \to \mathbb{P}(\mathcal{Q})\)
316	  (EGA IV 17.16.3 (ii)) whose composition with the universal divisor map
317	  \(\mathbb{P}(\mathcal{Q}) \to Z\) produces the required \(T_1\)-point of \(Z\).
318	  Moreover, the first projection \(Z \times_{P^{\phi_0}_0} Z \to Z\) is
319	  smooth and proper.
320	
321	  \emph{Step 4: descend by the smooth-proper quotient lemma.}
322	  Apply Kleiman~\S 4, Lem.~4.9: given a surjection \(\alpha : Z \to P\)
323	  of \'etale sheaves with \(Z\) quasi-projective, \(R := Z \times_P Z\)
324	  representable, and the first projection \(R \to Z\) smooth and proper,
325	  \(P\) is representable by a quasi-projective scheme. In our case
326	  \(P = P^{\phi_0}_0\), \(Z\) and \(R\) as above, so \(P^{\phi_0}_0\) is
327	  quasi-projective. Reassembling over \(\phi \in \mathbb{Q}[n]\) and
328	  over \(m\), the disjoint union of representable \(P^\phi\) pieces represents
329	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\). The total scheme is separated (as a disjoint
330	  union of separated quasi-projectives) and locally of finite type. This
331	  is Kleiman~\S 4, Cor.~4.18.3 in the \(S = \Spec k\),
332	  \(X = C\) complete case.
333	\end{proof}
334	
335	\subsection{The smooth-proper quotient lemma}
336	\label{subsec:fga_pic_qt}
337	
338	The structural lemma underlying step 4 above is recorded separately
339	because it is consumed verbatim in the assembly proof: it is the
340	abstract content allowing the descent from \(Z = \mathrm{Div}^{\phi_0}_0\) to the
341	representing Picard piece.
342	
343	\begin{lemma}\leanok
344	[Smooth-proper quotient of \'etale sheaves]
345	  \label{lem:smooth_proper_quotient}
346	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.smoothProperQuotient}
347	  \uses{def:has_smooth_proper_quotient}
348	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4, Lem.~lm:qt;
349	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~31 (read from
350	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2368-L2375).
351	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
352	  % "\begin{lem}\label{lm:qt}
353	  %   Let \(\alpha\:Z\to P\) be a map of \'etale sheaves, and set \(R:=Z\x_PZ\).
354	  %  Assume \(\alpha\) is a surjection, \(Z\) is representable by a
355	  %  quasi-projective \(S\)-scheme, \(R\) is representable by an \(S\)-scheme, and
356	  %  the first projection \(R\to Z\) is representable by a smooth and proper
357	  %  map.  Then \(P\) is representable by a quasi-projective \(S\)-scheme, and
358	  %  \(\alpha\) is representable by a smooth map."
359	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4, Lem.~4.9.}
360	  Let \(\alpha : Z \to P\) be a morphism of \'etale sheaves on \((\Sch/k)\).
361	  Set \(R := Z \times_P Z\). Suppose:
362	  \begin{enumerate}
363	    \item \(\alpha\) is a surjection of \'etale sheaves;
364	    \item \(Z\) is representable by a quasi-projective \(k\)-scheme;
365	    \item \(R\) is representable by a \(k\)-scheme;
366	    \item the first projection \(R \to Z\) is representable by a smooth
367	      and proper map.
368	  \end{enumerate}
369	  Then \(P\) is representable by a quasi-projective \(k\)-scheme, and \(\alpha\)
370	  is representable by a smooth map.
371	\end{lemma}
372	
373	% NOTE (run-0008 truth audit): the LEAN statement of smoothProperQuotient is
374	% intentionally WEAKER than this blueprint statement: hypothesis (2)'s
375	% quasi-projectivity of Z is not expressible at the pinned Mathlib revision
376	% (no QuasiProjective vocabulary), and without it the naked statement is
377	% FALSE (a Hironaka-type free Z/2-action on a smooth proper non-projective
378	% 3-fold gives a smooth proper equivalence relation whose etale quotient
379	% sheaf is a non-scheme algebraic space). The Lean theorem therefore takes
380	% the conclusion-carrier class HasSmoothProperQuotient (def:has_smooth_proper_quotient)
381	% as a USE-SITE hypothesis, standing in for the missing quasi-projectivity;
382	% the former GLOBAL ⟨sorry⟩ instance (which asserted representability of
383	% EVERY presheaf target and was flatly false) was deleted in run 0008.
384	% Supplying the instance for the Abel-map slice (where Z is a genuinely
385	% quasi-projective open of Div_{C/k}) is part of the inst_has_pic_scheme
386	% obligation. Consequently the PROOF below is NOT formalised (no \leanok on
387	% the proof): it is the mathematical justification for the use-site
388	% instance, not for the Lean theorem body (which is a one-line extraction).
389	
390	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex,
391	% L2377-L2417, proof of lm:qt; reproduced verbatim because it is the
392	% structural heart of step 4.)
393	% "To lighten the notation, let \(Z\) and \(R\) denote the
394	% corresponding schemes as well.  Since the structure map \(Z\to S\) is
395	% quasi-projective, it is separated; whence, the first projection
396	% \(Z\x_SZ\to Z\) is too.  But the first projection \(R\to Z\) is proper, and
397	% factors naturally through a map \(h\:R\to Z\x_SZ\).  Hence \(h\) is proper.
398	% But \(h\) is a monomorphism; that is, \(h\) is injective on \(T\)-points.
399	% Therefore, \(h\) is a closed embedding by \cite[8.11.5]{EGAIV3}.
400	%
401	% Plainly, for each \(S\)-scheme \(T\), the subset $R(T)\subset
402	% Z(T)\x_{S(T)}Z(T)$ is the graph of an equivalence relation.  Also, the
403	% map of schemes \(R\to Z\) is flat and proper, and \(Z\) is a
404	% quasi-projective \(S\)-scheme.  It follows that there exist a
405	% quasi-projective \(S\)-scheme \(Q\) and a faithfully flat and projective map
406	% \(Z\to Q\) such that \(R=Z\x_QZ\).  (In other words, a flat and proper
407	% equivalence relation on a quasi-projective scheme is effective.)
408	% [\dots]
409	% Since \(Z\to Q\) is flat, it is smooth if (and only if) its fibers are
410	% smooth.  But these fibers are, up to extension of the ground field, the
411	% same as those of \(R\to Z\).  And \(R\to Z\) is smooth by hypothesis.  Thus
412	% \(Z\to Q\) is smooth.
413	%
414	% It remains to see that \(Q\) represents \(P\).  First, observe that \(Z\to Q\)
415	% is (represents) a surjection of \'etale sheaves.
416	% [\dots]
417	% Since \(Z\to Q\) is a surjection of \'etale sheaves, \(Q\) is, in the
418	% category of \'etale sheaves, the coequalizer of the pair of maps
419	% \(R\rightrightarrows Z\) by Exercise~\ref{ex:epi} below, which is an
420	% elementary exercise in general Grothendieck topology.  By the same
421	% exercise, \(P\) too is the coequalizer of this pair of maps.  But, in any
422	% category, the coequalizer is unique up to unique isomorphism.  Thus \(Q\)
423	% represents \(P\)."
424	\begin{proof}
425	  By (ii) the structure map \(Z \to \Spec k\) is quasi-projective, hence
426	  separated, so the first projection \(Z \times_k Z \to Z\) is separated.
427	  By (iv) the first projection \(R \to Z\) is proper; it factors through
428	  \(h : R \to Z \times_k Z\) over the diagonal embedding (the second
429	  component is the second projection of \(R \to Z \times_P Z\)). The map
430	  \(h\) is therefore proper, and a monomorphism (because \(R\) is the graph
431	  of an equivalence relation on \(Z\), hence injective on \(T\)-points).
432	  By EGA IV 8.11.5, a proper monomorphism is a closed immersion: \(h\) is a
433	  closed embedding.
434	
435	  Hence \(R \hookrightarrow Z \times_k Z\) is a flat (by (iv)) and proper
436	  equivalence relation on the quasi-projective \(k\)-scheme \(Z\). By the
437	  theory of flat-and-proper effective equivalence relations on
438	  quasi-projective schemes (Altman--Kleiman; the construction places
439	  the graph \(R \subseteq Z \times_k Z\) inside the universal subscheme of
440	  the Hilbert scheme \(\Hilb_{Z/k}\) and descends to a closed subscheme
441	  \(Q \subseteq \Hilb_{Z/k}\) representing the quotient), there exist a
442	  quasi-projective \(k\)-scheme \(Q\) and a faithfully flat, projective map
443	  \(Z \to Q\) with \(R = Z \times_Q Z\).
444	
445	  The map \(Z \to Q\) is flat and proper; its fibers, up to field
446	  extension, coincide with those of \(R \to Z\), which are smooth by
447	  hypothesis. Hence \(Z \to Q\) is smooth.
448	
449	  Finally, \(Z \to Q\) is a surjection of \'etale sheaves (an \'etale
450	  cover of any \(T\)-point of \(Q\) pulls back to a \(T'\)-point of \(Z\), by
451	  EGA IV 17.16.3 (ii) applied to \(Z \times_Q T \to T\)). In the category
452	  of \'etale sheaves, both \(Q\) and \(P\) are coequalisers of the pair
453	  \(R \rightrightarrows Z\); the coequaliser is unique up to unique
454	  isomorphism, so \(Q\) represents \(P\). Smoothness of \(\alpha : Z \to P\)
455	  is the smoothness of \(Z \to Q\) just shown.
456	\end{proof}
